title: read_text_file offset_arg>0 check uses raw u64 not the post-saturating-sub usize
description:
## Severity
Trivial

## Dimension
Functional Correctness / Naming

## Location
src/tools/file/read.rs:273-316 (read_text_file offset/limit handling)

## Violation
The function extracts `offset` from args, computes `offset = offset_arg.saturating_sub(1)`, and later uses `offset > 0 || limit.is_some()` to decide whether to append the "showing lines ..." suffix. The test read_text_offset_zero_treated_as_start_of_file (line 418-432) pins the observation that "OC produces a suffix even when offset=0 because offset_arg>0 check uses the pre-subtraction value — this is an OC quirk."

The function does NOT actually do this — the check `if offset > 0 || limit.is_some()` (line 310) uses `offset` AFTER saturating_sub. But the test COMMENT documents an intent that does not match the code. Either the test comment is stale (CC parity gap that no longer exists) or there's a related subtle bug — but either way, the comment misleads future maintainers.

This is the "stale comment" anti-pattern — comments are documentation that the compiler does not check. They drift.

## Mandated refactor
1. Audit the test comment and either delete it or fix the code to match the documented intent. Confirm via a fresh test what suffix-emission semantic OC actually has and pin that exactly.
2. Replace the `(u64 → usize)` saturating subtraction logic with a clearer pattern: model offset as `Option<NonZeroU64>` — None means "from start," Some(n) means "skip n-1 lines." Use `.map_or(0, |n| n.get() - 1)` for the skip count. This eliminates the saturating_sub indirection AND makes 0-vs-absent distinguishable.

## Filed from QA review dispatch
